ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301103 covers brewed oolong tea as a category; no specific entry exists for Lishan high-mountain oolong drip-bag format. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers oolong tea infusion generically; no Lishan-specific or drip-bag-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 covers oolong tea as a substance; no drip-bag or Lishan-specific concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.description: 1 drip tea bag brewed in approximately 200 ml hot water
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is one 4 g Lishan Oolong drip tea bag brewed in approximately 200 ml of hot water, yielding approximately 200 g of brewed tea beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, oolong, brewed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 14366)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Lishan oolong is a high-mountain Taiwanese oolong. No dedicated entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific tea. USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed oolong tea (NDB 14366) is used as the closest approved secondary source. Values represent the brewed liquid per 100 g. The dry tea bag (4 g) yields a brewed beverage whose nutrient profile per 100 g is effectively that of brewed oolong tea. Caffeine and polyphenol content are characteristic of high-mountain oolong but are not separately tracked in this nutrient schema.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Beverages — Plain brewed tea (oolong, green, black) consensus; University of Sydney GI Database category reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed oolong tea contains negligible fermentable carbohydrates (approximately 0.1 g per 100 g brewed liquid). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently classify plain unsweetened teas as having no measurable glycemic response. GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) per Project Veritas convention for zero-glycemic beverages. GL per serving is 0.0 because available carbohydrate per serving is effectively zero. Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283 — plain tea is not listed because it elicits no glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content (approximately 3 mg per 100 g brewed tea)
hypertension.guidance: Plain brewed Lishan oolong tea contains negligible sodium and no added salt, making it a suitable beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension; however, patients should avoid adding sugar syrups or high-sodium additives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and no glycemic impact when consumed plain and unsweetened
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain brewed Lishan oolong tea contains approximately 0.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g and exerts no measurable glycemic effect, making it an appropriate beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes provided no sugar or sweetened syrups are added; polyphenols in oolong tea have been associated with modest improvements in insulin sensitivity in some observational studies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of tea polyphenols with antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain brewed Lishan oolong tea is free of saturated fat and cholesterol and contains tea polyphenols that have been associated with antioxidant and cardioprotective effects in epidemiological studies; it is a heart-friendly beverage choice when consumed without added sugar or cream.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lishan Oolong Drip Tea Bag (梨山乌龙茶挂耳包) is a premium single-serve pour-over tea product crafted by CHICHA San Chen, a celebrated Taiwanese specialty tea brand with outlets across Asia including Singapore. Each sealed 4-gram drip bag contains carefully hand-selected whole Lishan high-mountain oolong tea leaves, chosen for their exceptional quality and aromatic complexity. Lishan, meaning 'Pear Mountain,' is one of Taiwan's most prestigious high-altitude tea-growing regions, situated above 1,800 metres in the central mountain range of Taiwan. The unique terroir of Lishan — characterised by cool temperatures averaging 10–15 °C during the growing season, persistent mountain mist, abundant rainfall, and mineral-rich volcanic soil — imparts a distinctive depth and sweetness to the tea leaves that is impossible to replicate at lower elevations. This high-altitude cultivation slows leaf growth, concentrating flavour compounds, polyphenols, and natural sugars within the leaf. The oolong leaves are harvested by hand and undergo a meticulous light-to-medium oxidation process (approximately 20–30% oxidation) followed by a gentle roast. This careful processing preserves the tea's inherent fresh, floral, and fruity character — with aromas reminiscent of orchids, osmanthus, and ripe stone fruit — while introducing a delicate underlying toastiness and a lingering natural sweetness on the finish. The drip-bag (挂耳包) format is a modern innovation in Taiwanese tea culture that bridges traditional gongfu brewing and contemporary convenience. To prepare, the drip bag is suspended over a cup using its built-in ear hooks, and hot water at 85–95 °C is poured slowly through the leaves in two to three controlled stages of approximately 50–70 ml each, allowing optimal extraction of the tea's layered flavours and volatile aromatic compounds. This method yields approximately 150–200 ml of a clear, pale golden-amber beverage with a light, silky body, a clean floral fragrance, and a smooth, mellow taste that evolves pleasantly on the palate with a sweet, lingering aftertaste. Nutritionally, brewed plain Lishan oolong tea is an essentially calorie-free beverage, providing only approximately 1 kcal per 100 g of brewed liquid. It contains naturally zero protein and naturally zero fat, with only trace carbohydrates (approximately 0.1 g per 100 g), confirmed zero sugar, and confirmed zero dietary fibre in the brewed liquid (fibre remains in the spent leaves). Sodium content is negligible at approximately 3 mg per 100 g. The beverage is rich in beneficial polyphenolic compounds — including catechins, theaflavins, and thearubigins — which contribute to its antioxidant properties and have been studied for potential roles in metabolic health. For individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, plain unsweetened Lishan oolong tea exerts no measurable glycemic effect (GI effectively zero, GL = 0.0 per serving) and may support insulin sensitivity based on observational evidence. For those with hypertension, its negligible sodium content makes it a safe daily beverage. For cardiovascular health, the absence of saturated fat and cholesterol combined with polyphenol antioxidants supports its classification as a heart-friendly drink. The moderate caffeine content (approximately 30–50 mg per 200 ml serving) provides a gentle, sustained stimulant effect without the abruptness associated with coffee. The drip-bag format makes this premium high-mountain oolong accessible in office, travel, and home settings without specialised brewing equipment, representing a convergence of Taiwanese tea craftsmanship and modern lifestyle convenience.

confidence_reason: No dedicated nutritional entry exists for Lishan oolong drip tea in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA Branded Foods. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy brewed oolong tea (NDB 14366), which is the closest approved secondary source. The brewed beverage profile is well-established and consistent across sources. GI is estimated by analogy as plain unsweetened tea has no measurable glycemic response. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact product formulation and brewing yield have not been independently verified against a primary source specific to this product.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the brewed beverage per 100 g, not the dry tea leaf. The serving size is approximately 200 g of brewed tea from one 4 g drip bag. 2. protein_g is naturally zero as brewed plain oolong tea contains no appreciable protein. fat_g is naturally zero as brewed plain oolong tea contains no fat. sugar_g is confirmed zero for plain unsweetened brewed oolong tea per USDA FoodData Central. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero for the brewed liquid (fibre remains in the spent tea leaves). 3. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for plain unsweetened teas. GL per serving is 0.0 because available carbohydrate per 200 g serving is approximately 0.2 g, yielding a GL of zero. 4. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed oolong tea. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 5. This product is sold by CHICHA San Chen at One Fullerton, Singapore, via field survey at SGD 4.95 per bag. 6. Multilingual naming: this is a Chinese-origin Taiwanese tea product sold in Singapore. Chinese simplified and traditional names are provided. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean dish; the cuisine is classified as Chinese (Taiwanese). G6 multilingual compliance applies to Singaporean dishes; this product is Chinese cuisine and requires Chinese-script coverage, which is satisfied.
